data.title: PPG Industries: Price Hikes Will Help Stabilize Margins
data.description:
It’s been around a year since my first article on PPG Industries, Inc. (PPG), so there is no better time to look at the company’s FY25 performance and what is still in store for 2026.

Briefly on Performance

For the first half of 2025, the company struggled to grow its top line. FX headwinds, as well as divestitures, affected the company’s performance throughout the first two quarters. From the third quarter onwards, the previous divestitures were out of the way, with organic sales rising by over 2%, pushed higher by stronger aerospace, protective, and marine demand. The last quarter of the year was particularly good, with 5% growth y/y, 3% organic growth, with higher selling prices and sales volumes across all regions.

Over the last year, the company’s profitability took a dip, unfortunately. Global architectural coatings was the biggest drag early in the year, with Q1 sales down due to FX and lower volumes, with EBITDA margins falling to 16.8% from 19.9%. Another drag in Q1 was the Industrial coatings segment, with EBITDA decreasing by 90bps. The segment that slightly helped offset the weakness was the Performance coating segment, which not only maintained its EBITDA, but even increased it by 20bps. Q2 showed very similar performance across the segments.

It’s only in Q4 that we see a marked improvement once again in the two underperforming segments, but nothing comes easy, as the Performance Coatings segment saw a decent dip in profitability. Segment’s EBITDA went from 23.1% to 21.1%. Automotive was the culprit behind the underperformance. Overall, FY25 saw around 80bps decrease in profitability.

Looking at other key profitability metrics, we can see that, with the decent boost in the bottom line, PPG’s efficiency has vastly improved as well, as evidenced by an increase across all three metrics below. The management is much more efficient at utilizing the company’s assets, as well as shareholder capital. ROTC is close to my minimum of 10%, so if the momentum continues, PPG will be quite attractive.

Going over the company’s financial position, PPG finished the year with over $2.1B in cash and equivalents against $6.6B in long-term debt, which is quite a lot. But is it worrisome? I don’t think so. The company’s interest coverage ratio for the year was 9x, which is well above what most analysts think is healthy (2x) and also higher than what I am looking for, which is 5x.

Also, the company’s cash flow has improved over the last year as well. Cash from operations was close to $2B, with free cash flow at around $1.16B vs. $669M last year. The company is a cash machine even as profitability declined, and top-line performance was quite lackluster.

Preliminary Results

PPG went ahead and pre-announced its first-quarter EPS numbers that increased around 6% over last year’s Q1 to $1.83. I would say the real reason for pre-announcing is that they had big news to share. PPG said that they are looking to increase prices by up to 20% across all product lines due to increased cost pressures and supply constraints.

What this means for the company.

That is quite a decent price hike. I wouldn’t say this tool is looking to increase margins and profitability for the sake of it. We saw that the company’s profitability took a hit in 2025, so I view it as more of a defensive and corrective tool. The company is trying to push through higher costs, protect supply continuity, and restore its margins after a disappointing year like 2025, when its operating leverage weakened.

I doubt a 20% increase is going to flow through to the bottom line. There will be some customer pushback, timing lags, and contracts in place before the hike was introduced, so the increase will flow through gradually.

Additionally, the company is still dealing with soft industrial and European demand, and the expectation of these segments is that they will remain challenged a little while longer. It makes sense to hike prices; the company has the pricing power and better end-market exposure. For instance, aerospace is one positive I can see that will help its operations continue to grow.

With the release of Q1 ’26 numbers looming on April 28, I will be looking out for more details on how the operations will develop with the price hikes in place for the rest of 2026. Should we expect a guidance adjustment to the upside? I wouldn’t rule it out. In the pre-announcement, the company said that it expects second-quarter organic sales and adjusted earnings to be flat to low single-digit percentage growth, but I would like to hear about Q3 and Q4, as that is where we are going to get most of the flow-through of the price hikes.

In terms of other important growth factors, I am going to look for how the company’s performance coatings come in for Q1, after a marked decline in Q4. Also, I will be looking out for the strength in the other two segments to persist into Q1 and not revert to declines as we saw in Q1 and Q2 last year. It’s a possibility that they will.

As I mentioned earlier, European industrial demand and broader industrial markets have been a drag recently, so I would like to hear some comments on these areas to gauge what we should expect going forward. They already said that they expect them to remain subdued, but any indication of recovery is welcome.

Aerospace has been the one decent compounder and a savior for the company over the last while, so I would like to see this end market remain resilient going forward. High utilization, specification, and recurring maintenance demand are what make aerospace so attractive. The opportunity comes from two places: new aircraft production and maintenance/repaint demand. Airline traffic is poised for growth in 2026, and with last year’s announcement of a $380m aerospace coatings and sealants facility, the demand is there, as the company’s backlog in the segment continues to grow.

Should you buy PPG?

I am looking to sit this one out, given the PPG share price performance over the last year. I don’t see much growth yet, but I will be looking out for what kind of effect the price hike is going to have in the long run. Usually, when many manufacturers raise prices, they hardly drop them again, even as costs decline, unless the competition begins to cut and undermine them. So, I will monitor the margin progress going forward, but I don’t think there is an opportunity for me just yet.

data.title: Figma: Is It Finally Time? Partially (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Figma, Inc. (FIG) shares have collapsed 83% in less than a year after a post-IPO bounce that took the company's valuation to unprecedented overvaluation territory.

I warned investors that the fall will be steep and that it's important to wait patiently for a real, fundamentally sound valuation, as even after the initial 70% fall, I found the stock to be extremely overvalued.

As the stock has fallen another 47% since, investors are once again asking, is it finally time? And my answer is, simply, no. Well, partially.

Figma's Growth Shows All Arrows Point Towards The Right Direction

Figma continues to grow at a rapid pace, exceeding consensus estimates on both revenue and non-GAAP EPS for the third consecutive quarter as a public company.

Revenue in Q4 rose 40% to a record $304 million, reflecting a nice 2-point acceleration from 38% growth in Q3.

Net dollar retention rate also expanded to 136%, the highest level of the past two years.

Moreover, Figma started disclosing its gross retention rate, which is a far more important metric to the “terminal value” discussion, as it shows the actual churn of customers (unlike net dollar retention, where one customer's expansion offsets another customer's churn). Of note, the 97% mark is quite strong and appears to be in line with the previous seven quarters.

Another angle that shows strong growth is in the number of significant paying customers, with the $10,000+ cohort expanding by 951 customers and the $100,000 group expanding by 143. If you want to nitpick, this is somewhat of a deceleration, but not a meaningful one, and it depends on which quarter you compare it to.

Looking ahead, Figma guided for 38% growth in Q1-26 and 30% growth in FY26. While this reflects a slowdown from the 41% level in 2025, this is still an impressive level, and it came above consensus expectations.

Except For The Most Crucial Part - Profits

I think we can say that Figma's growth remains exceptional and that, as far as the numbers show, there are no AI existential headwinds to speak of. To me, I'm much more concerned because of something else, and that's profits.

Non-GAAP gross margins continue to drop as Figma's AI offerings, which are margin-dilutive, gain traction. Management said they are consistently reducing their cost-to-serve per user, but the difference in margin between an AI offering and a legacy software solution is likely meaningful. In other words, there's a long way to go before we find out the representative margin rate.

While AI is supposed to be a productivity enhancer, so far, what we're seeing is that for Figma, it's mainly a top-line driver but a net negative on productivity and margins. Gross margins dropped 6 points Y/Y, and non-GAAP operating margins dropped 12 points, with management citing investments in people, infrastructure, and systems.

For 2026, they expect to deliver $105 million of non-GAAP operating income at the midpoint, which would reflect 7.7% margins and another step lower in margins relative to the 12% achieved in 2026.

All that margin talk and I didn't mention perhaps the most critical part: we are, of course, talking about non-GAAP margins.

In Figma's case, those non-GAAP adjustments primarily include stock-based compensation, at a jaw-dropping amount, to be frank, and $1.3 billion, to be exact. If we assume the same level of SBC in 2026, we're talking about a $1.2 billion operating loss instead of a $105 million profit. And, if we assume the run rate of Q4, we're talking about a $700 million loss.

Either way, it's hard to make the case that Figma is some type of value play here, after falling 83%.

Valuation Remains Stretched Based On Fundamentals

Because Figma is far from generating real profits or cash flows, we can't really evaluate it based on traditional fundamentals.

One way to look at it is adjusted EPS, which puts Figma at 87 times forward earnings and 71 times next year's estimates.

This is far above other software names, but Figma is growing two or three times as fast as some of them, so based on a PEG ratio, the gap isn't as high, still very high though.

Another way to look at valuation is based on revenues, where Figma trades at 6.6 times forward sales:

This also places them far above most software names, but again, not relative to the expected growth.

Once a real fundamental valuation is no longer an option, there's not much to get excited about here in my view, but there is a legitimate case to be made that Figma's stock is getting closer to reasonable levels. Still not all the way there, though.

Conclusion

Even after the 83% fall, Figma is objectively far from being attractively valued based on fundamentals. Meanwhile, it's hard to take a leap of faith and bet on the company's long-term profit potential when margins keep contracting and AI creates significant uncertainties.

That said, gross retention is exceptionally strong, growth-adjusted non-GAAP multiples are at reasonable levels, and Figma has shown it can deliver frontier AI-based solutions to its customers.

At $20 per share, I no longer view Figma as a 'Sell,' but it's still not attractive to me. As such, I upgrade the stock to 'Hold.'

data.title: ECB Set To Signal An 'Insurance' Rate Hike
data.description:
By Peter Vanden Houte, Chief Economist, Belgium, Luxembourg, Eurozone

Slower growth ahead

As the war in the Middle East lingers on and a quick normalisation of energy prices looks unlikely, the stagflationary impact of the crisis is likely to manifest itself in a more pronounced way in the coming months. Before the war, activity data already pointed to a subdued growth profile. Industrial production growth remained subdued in the first two months of the year, and eurozone retail sales stagnated. In March, both business and consumer sentiment soured further.

Leading indicators like new orders and hiring intentions in March’s business sentiment indicators foreshadow slower growth in the months ahead. With unemployment expectations rising, we cannot count on a substantial decline in the savings ratio to boost household spending. And while countries like Spain, Italy, and Germany have already announced some measures to counter the rise in energy prices, the total stimulus is likely to remain much lower than in 2022-23.

Growth expectations downgraded again

In the ECB’s March staff projections, several scenarios were explored, with the adverse and severe scenarios indicating GDP growth rates of 0.6% and 0.4%, respectively. We are leaning toward the adverse scenario, now forecasting 0.7% growth for 2026, down from 0.9%. Nevertheless, German fiscal stimulus hasn’t vanished and, together with falling energy prices next year, should spark a renewed recovery in 2027. As a result, growth for 2027 is expected to reach 1.3%, slightly less than the prior estimate of 1.4%.

Inflation heading towards 4%

HICP inflation climbed to 2.5% in March from 1.9% in February, driven by higher energy costs. Core inflation is still under control for now, with the annualised three-month change in consumer prices excluding energy and food steady at 2%. Still, some secondary inflation effects are likely since businesses are signalling increased selling price expectations in surveys. We anticipate headline inflation rising towards 4% in the coming months, while core inflation is likely to hover between 2.5% and 3.0% in the second half of the year. This should produce an average headline inflation of 3.4% in 2026 and 2.4% in 2027.

An insurance rate hike

Although the ECB currently has limited data to act upon, financial markets expect at least two rate hikes this year. We know that a supply shock is the hardest to tackle for a central bank. As a matter of fact, rate hikes will not boost the crude oil supply.

However, the ECB is unwilling to risk inflation expectations deteriorating as it did in 2022-23. Unlike that period, the deposit rate is now at 2%, compared to -0.5% at the start of 2022. This suggests that a modest rate hike would probably suffice as a signal. Call it an "insurance" rate hike. Could we see two rate increases? It’s possible, but we continue to believe the ECB tightening will fall short of market expectations.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Harbour Energy: Excellent Acquisition Timing
data.description:
Harbour Energy (PMOIF)(HBRIY) made a big acquisition covered in the last article that closed in February. One thing about investment grade ideas is that these companies tend to get unusually lucky when it comes to making a leveraged deal. In this case, more than two billion dollars of debt was part of the deal. But the Iranian situation is making this additional leverage look like a brilliant move. The company is now in an excellent position (despite hedging) to use the extra cash flow resulting from high commodity prices to repay the debt that resulted from the acquisition.

It should also be noted that many acquisitions have a significant amount of one-time (and optimization) costs. This company may well have extra downside protection when the time comes for commodity prices to retreat thanks to that extra debt (that is likely to get repaid at least somewhat early).

It also mitigates that the company was disclosing a negative outlook from most of the rating firms due to the additional debt obtained through the acquisition. This is one of the very few times that the additional debt taken on to complete the merger happens to be a big plus. As a result, that negative outlook is likely to get quickly removed as part of the debt rating.

Rising commodity prices often have an outsized effect on earnings because the extra money obtained from rising commodity prices often heads to the bottom line with few deductions. The costs were already paid for (so to speak) when commodity prices were lower. So, there are not all that many additional costs when commodity prices rise.

There is some reduction in exposure due to the hedging. But I have yet to see a company that hedges more than 80% of its production. That does leave some exposure to commodity price fluctuations no matter the hedging program.

Acquisitions

The company has one additional smaller acquisition to complete that will liberate a fair amount of tax losses that the company can use.

As shown above, the company has been selling anything considered noncore in an attempt to high-grade the portfolio. Earlier it was mentioned that the company also exited Vietnam as well.

Operations Map

As a result, the company has operations that are not affected by the Iranian situation.

The net result of the transactions is that the company is significantly larger with help from higher commodity prices. There are much worse things that could happen than this. Of all the companies that I follow, this one is ideally positioned to take advantage of the higher commodity prices without having to worry about damaged operations (at least so far) due to the Iranian situation.

Whatever needs to happen both financially and operationally, is going to happen with likely higher commodity prices than originally forecast (or likely used when negotiating the transactions). Overall, this company could emerge from the current period of high prices with a much improved situation.

The location of much of the production near to Europe is also a very favorable competitive advantage compared to much of the industry that has to ship the production from further away. That helps profitability as well.

Now there is a surcharge on excess profits in some locations that may well decrease some of that location advantage. On the other hand, there should still be money leftover.

Taxes

One of the more unusual ideas presented here is a margin expansion based upon a migration of production to lower tax areas.

Not only is the company materially different in the current fiscal year due to the portfolio shuffling, but it is also different due to plans to lower the corporate tax rate by shifting production over time to lower tax areas.

That may well make the past history irrelevant to future results. In fact, it could be very interesting to see how the results are changing based upon the transactions management has done recently.

Summary

This management improved free cash flow materially despite lower commodity prices in fiscal year 2025.

A management that improves free cash flow during a period of declining commodity prices is a very rare management. Just imagine what this management can do with higher commodity prices combined with the envisioned improvements (that were on the agenda at lower forecast commodity prices). This company could well be in a position to "print money" in the coming fiscal year.

This idea clearly remains a strong buy even accounting for the fact that we have no idea about the future of commodity prices. The current management has got the company in a growth mode that is likely to last for the foreseeable future.

Debt

This company operates in a high tax country (or countries). Therefore, reporting a loss after taxes is not that unusual despite the investment grade rating.

What should be important is the investment grade rating of the debt (even with the negative outlook), where the major reason is decent cash flow as shown above.

The actual debt ratio was well under 1.0 before the recent acquisition. It remains to be seen how the higher commodity prices translate into improved performance (in addition to the portfolio moves made for improved performance).

Reported losses and all, this is actually a fairly healthy company that pays a dividend and is likely to continue to pay a dividend. It is true, though, that operating in higher tax countries does make for interesting tax strategies.

Dividend Payout

Before the higher commodity prices happened, management was warning that until the debt levels declined sufficiently, the payout ratio would likely be closer to 40% of cash flow.

Even if the company stays with that "near 40%" of cash flow idea, the cash flow could be materially higher due to the higher commodity prices. So, this whole slide could get very interesting as the fiscal year progresses.

Right now, the whole project would appear to be very conservative. But then again, future commodity prices are both low visibility and very volatile. Therefore, let us see how the year unfolds.

But I would consider holding something like this as long as management continues to add value. Even if the commodity prices retreat, this management would appear to be growing the company for the foreseeable future. That provides long-term downside protection even if there turns out to be a common stock price retreat somewhere in the future.

Risks

Anytime, a company grows through acquisition, there is always the risk that one or more acquisitions proves to be a disappointment. Such an event could set back the company's growth plans for a period of time. So far, this company has a very good record in that area. But there is absolutely no guarantee that record will continue into the future.

A severe and sustained commodity price downturn could materially change the company outlook. That is particularly true when the company has a goal to reduce debt. In this case, the company got very lucky with higher commodity prices after making an acquisition using a lot of debt. Now let us see how long that luck holds out and what the effects of that luck are.

Managements that do what this management is doing are a very small subset of everyone out there that can do any given job in the company. Therefore, a loss of services of key people could prove a big challenge to replace. What may happen instead is a change to the company's future outlook for a period of time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fuchs Presents Heavily Sandbagged Growth Targets
data.description:
Fuchs 100 growth targets

Probably nobody expected the world’s largest independent lubricant manufacturer, Fuchs (FUPEF) (FUPBY), to change its longstanding strategy together with its CEO, but markets were still curious to see the future CEO’s long-term goals. Since the previous 2025 goals were missed due to COVID and geopolitical volatility, Fuchs had already communicated that the next long-term targets would include some caveats regarding raw material inflation, forex, etc., and would be updated regularly as general conditions change.

In 2031 Fuchs will celebrate its 100th birthday, which makes the year an ideal endpoint for a long-term outlook. The goals indicated today look a bit disappointing at first sight:

Sales revenues in the range of EUR 4.0-4.5 billion

EBIT in the range of EUR 550-600 million

EBIT margin in the range of 13-15%

The targets of an annual dividend increase and a cash conversion rate of 0.8 were already achieved during the FUCHS2025 strategy cycle and will remain in place under the FUCHS100 strategy.

Starting from 2025 EBIT of €433 million, this roughly translates into a target CAGR of 5%.

However, there is another big caveat in the slide set:

“FUCHS100 targets are based on flat development for both material costs and sales prices”—i.e., the 5% CAGR represents real growth, and inflation would add to that, as Fuchs has always managed to pass on raw material inflation.

Additionally, since only about 50% of net income is paid out as dividends and M&A is not expected to be huge (there are just too few available targets), there will almost certainly be buybacks, further enhancing EPS growth. Clearly, any significant M&A transaction should also increase EPS, as all growth targets refer to organic growth.

Last but not least, there will be lower tax rates for German businesses from 2028.

As a result, I expect the relatively modest EBIT growth CAGR of 5% to translate into an EPS growth of about 6-7% + some inflation adjustment until 2031.

This is probably a little bit more than consensus expectations.

Moreover, during today’s Q&A session, the CFO underscored what I had evidenced in my introductory thesis: Having just completed a large capital investment cycle, Fuchs enjoys a tailwind over the next few years, as less capital will be needed and utilization of the newly built capacity improves, which is why the company is confident it will reach its 15% EBIT margin target once general market conditions stabilize.

Overall, after the missed 2025 goals, the company appears to have heavily sandbagged its guidance.

Where growth will come from

Growth will mainly come from specialty applications and the automotive aftermarket, which is the higher-profitability part of the lubricants market. Fuchs will try to achieve this by focusing on those higher-margin areas where it has below-average market share: Automotive Aftermarket, Customer Brands, Rotary Motion, New Mobility, Performance Greases, and Special Application Solutions.

Overall, the strategy shows that the company will try to grow by doing more of the same, just a little better and with a tighter focus. So it seems a realistic, conservative, and achievable strategy.

Fuchs has always operated in stagnating or even shrinking markets and has an abundantly proven ability to increment its share in the most attractive areas of these markets.

Near-term considerations

During the presentation, the company also explained how it is dealing with the current disruption stemming from the war waged by the U.S. against Iran. The key focus of the company is to keep its current customers running. Order books are full, as everybody is trying to get as much product supply as possible, but Fuchs is not looking to acquire new customers in this scenario. It has already implemented two price increases and will probably do more rounds by year-end. In summary, it is keeping its EBIT guidance, although, with an inflation-driven sales increase, profit margins will obviously shrink. In addition, the current situation will require working capital increases. Fortunately, having a net cash position, the company doesn’t need any bank loans, which is always nice in times of crisis.

Valuation

Fuchs voting shares currently trade for 13.3x 2025 EPS and will pay out €1.22 in May, so we may argue it effectively trades for less than 13x EPS.

As highlighted in my larger introductory thesis last year, Fuchs is a far-above-average company with a pristine, net-cash balance sheet and a highly attractive, resilient niche business; it is superbly managed and, having just completed a major investment cycle, has little capital needs. It even remained profitable throughout the entire GFC, demonstrating the resilience of its business model.

Such a business, combined with a highly predictable growth trajectory in the same ballpark as the general market, in my view should be awarded an above-market multiple. Yet the opposite is the case—which makes the investment case even more attractive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 'Team Transitory' Holds For Now In The States
data.description:
By James Knightley, Chief International Economist, US

Inflation expectations remain tolerable

The Federal Reserve can’t do anything to ease the energy supply shock. Instead, its role is to ensure that inflation expectations remain contained, and, for now, that seems to be the case. Long-term consumer inflation expectations are tolerable, being a full percentage point below the 4.4% figure hit in the wake of the 'Liberation Day' tariffs 12 months ago. Meanwhile, break-even inflation rates on Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities have not shifted to a worrying degree – the 2Y implied rate is at 2.9%, 5Y at 2.7% and 10Y at 2.4%. In January, the averages were 2.5%, 2.4% and 2.3% respectively. As such, the central bank, households, and financial markets appear to believe that inflation is likely to be transitory.

While we expect US headline inflation to soon breach 4% on higher motor fuel and airline fare costs, we certainly don’t think we are in for a repeat of 2022, when inflation got within touching distance of 10%, and the Fed had to hike the policy rate by 525bp. That is primarily because the supply shock is not as broad as the pandemic-related supply chain stresses in 2022, focused on motor and aviation fuel. At the same time, we don’t have the same sort of demand impetus that would risk a broader, more persistent inflation story.

US companies have not been able to pass on the $25bn per month cost of tariffs to consumers in a meaningful way, with core goods consumer prices rising little more than 1% year-on-year. This gives us reason to doubt they will be able to pass on higher energy and distribution costs outside of airline fares. Furthermore, US natural gas prices have been falling, suggesting that developments in the Middle East will be less impactful on US utility costs than in Asia and Europe.

High energy costs likely to be demand destructive

In terms of activity, the differences with 2022 are even starker. The economy added 4.5 million jobs in 2022, wage growth averaged nearly 6%, savings had been built up during the pandemic, we had stimulus checks and significant pent-up demand as people sought a return to their pre-Covid lifestyles. Today, the jobs market has stalled, wage growth is closer to 3%, confidence is at record lows, savings have been depleted, and real household disposable income is flatlining. Even the tax refund story, which we had hoped would provide a near-term boost to spending power, is looking less supportive than predicted. After disappointing January and February consumer spending data, surging energy costs will eat into household spending power and risk being demand destructive; hence, our downward revision to 2026 GDP growth to 2.2% from 2.5%.

It isn’t all bad news. Net trade is making a positive contribution to growth while pipeline indicators for business investment remain strong, especially for tech-related business capital expenditure. Government spending should also provide a significant boost to first-quarter GDP growth after the October shutdown weighed heavily on fourth-quarter 2025 economic activity.

Dual mandate suggests rate cuts remain on the cards for late 2026

In terms of what this means for our call on Federal Reserve policy, we continue to believe interest rate cuts are more likely than hikes. With weaker consumer spending growth, core (excluding food and energy) price inflation is expected to be further dampened. In this regard, we need to remember the importance of shelter costs within the US inflation basket (35.5% weighting for headline and 44% for core), which we expect to moderate further.

The Federal Reserve's dual mandate of price stability and maximising employment is a key distinguishing feature of the US. Having failed to create jobs in meaningful numbers over the past 18 months, when growth was strong, the risk is that we see weaker US jobs figures on the back of geopolitical, financial market, and economic angst. Remember, too, that the Fed still views the current stance of monetary policy as being very modestly restrictive. With a new Fed governor taking office and key elections this year, the political pressure for action will intensify.

As such, if we do see Middle East tensions ease and oil prices drop back in the second half of this year, there is a strong chance of sub-2% inflation being achieved in 2027. So, while the Fed is likely to sound relatively hawkish in the near term, we continue to see scope for two 25bp rate cuts towards the end of the year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: American Eagle Outfitters' Shareholders Swoon For Sweeney
data.description:
April 15th was a really great day for shareholders of American Eagle Outfitters (AEO). Shares of the apparel company rose 9.3% after news broke that the business had expanded its partnership with Sydney Sweeney. The original partnership that it had with her helped to boost sales for the business. So the market views this expanded arrangement as one that should theoretically help sales even more. Honestly, investors should not be surprised by this move higher, though. The company has, ever since I called it a 'buy' back in October of last year, performed exceptionally well. While the S&P 500 is up 5.9%, shares of this business have risen 31.8%. And since I originally called it a 'buy' in February of last year, the stock has risen 41%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 14.8%.

This is not to say that everything is going great for the institution. There have been certain challenges, particularly on the bottom line. However, revenue is growing, and management expects a nice jump in profitability this year. Given this, and the fact that shares of the business still remain cheap on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms, I firmly believe that keeping it rated a 'buy' makes all the sense in the world here. This is not to say that the picture can't change as new data justifies. But honestly, quite a bit would have to go wrong in order for a downgrade to transpire.

A look at American Eagle Outfitters' mixed bag

Probably the best place to start with when it comes to looking at American Eagle Outfitters would be the data provided by management covering the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. These results came out on March 4th and indicated that revenue for the business amounted to $1.76 billion during that time. That's an increase of 9.7% over the $1.60 billion that the business reported a year earlier. According to management, this rise in revenue was driven largely by an 8% jump in comparable store sales. We did unfortunately see a decline in the number of locations in operation from 1,172 to 1,168. But that is a small drop in the grand scheme of things.

Management has been very clear about what has driven the upside that the business has enjoyed. For instance, as it continued to benefit from new product collections and fresh marketing campaigns, the company has seen a rise in demand. It enjoyed double-digit growth from both its Aerie and OFFLINE categories, and there was a bit of upside when it came to its hallmark American Eagle brand. According to the company, comparable sales growth for Aerie and OFFLINE during the quarter ended up being around 23%. That is fantastic if you ask me. Management seems to have done well when it comes to its strategy of leveraging partnerships with popular influencers. Even though Sidney Sweeney is the most famous example, management mentioned in their latest earnings call that they are gaining momentum with their influencer program and they are leveraging influencers across all of the brands in their portfolio. It also doesn't help that the company continues to make investments in remodeling and refreshing existing stores. This year, for instance, the firm said that they will do at least 60 of these, and they have between 350 and 400 that they can still focus on. The fact of the matter is that remodeled and refreshed stores draw in new customers. So to see success that has justified management continuing to move in that direction is promising.

As revenue increases, you would typically expect that profitability would rise also. But the picture here is a bit more nuanced. According to management, net profits actually fell from $104.3 million to $87.9 million year over year. This is disappointing to say the least. However, it was the result primarily of $84.5 million in impairment, restructuring, and other related charges. Most other profitability metrics for the business improved. Adjusted net profits, meanwhile, jumped from $121.4 million to $148.2 million. Operating cash flow, as an example, rose from $383.8 million to $415.9 million. Though if we do strip out changes in working capital, we would get a fall from $185.1 million to $181.5 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $199.3 million to $234.9 million. The chart above actually shows weakness across the board on the bottom line for 2025 even though revenue increased. So clearly, the final quarter of last year is a sign that the picture is turning around for the business.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management mentioned that comparable sales should rise at a mid-single-digit rate. The firm expects operating income of between $390 million and $410 million. Using a 21% income tax rate, and using other guidance figures provided by management, this should result in net profits of $309.5 million, adjusted operating cash flow of $534.5 million, and EBITDA totaling $618.5 million.

If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the company is valued not only on an absolute basis but also relative to other similar firms. Honestly, these are really attractive multiples if you ask me. In addition to being cheap on an absolute basis, the company is also cheap compared to other similar enterprises. Two of the five firms that I stacked it up against ended up being cheaper on a price-to-earnings basis. But when it came to both the price-to-operating cash flow approach and the EV-to-EBITDA approach, it ended up being the cheapest of the group.

It is likely, judging by how much the stock price rose on April 15th, that market participants now believe that financial performance could be even greater than what management forecasted for the year. I say this because, on April 15th, the company announced that it has partnered back up with Sydney Sweeney for a new brand summer campaign called Syd for Short: American Eagle Jean Shorts. The firm described this as an evolution of its partnership with Sydney Sweeney, with 'Syd' referring to a more casual dimension of Sydney Sweeney in the company's shorts during her relaxed, everyday life, including when she is at home, running errands, or at the beach. This arrangement also involves support for the Crisis Text Line, which is a nonprofit that offers 24/7 mental health support free of charge for those who need it. Certainly, this charitable side to it has appeal in its own right. And, for individuals that it touches on directly, it will likely serve as a draw to the company and its offerings.

The great news here is that the firm certainly has plenty to offer. Management said that their shorts destination features more than 200 shorts styles and washes across women's and men's. This summer will bring more than 850 new styles centered around laid-back, wear-anywhere pieces utilizing everything from relaxed denim to versatile tops. Customers can also shop Syd's pics, such as the Low-Rise Shortie and the Super Low-Rise Micro Skort. When you add on top of this other growth initiatives, such as the company's decision to increase the number of store openings for its Aerie and OFFLINE brands, there is a lot to be bullish about here.

Takeaway

The way I see it, things are looking rather interesting for American Eagle Outfitters right now. Management has demonstrated improvements in the bottom line. However, we need to have additional operating quarters before we can say that this is anything more than a blip on the radar. However, comparable sales growth and this new expanded partnership with Sydney Sweeney certainly bode well for the business. You also have other positives about the company. For instance, it has no debt on its books and enjoys a cash position of $238.9 million. So the company is certainly on solid footing in that regard. When you add all of this together, maintaining it as a 'buy' makes sense.

data.title: The Blockade: Stealth De-Escalation Is Working
data.description:
We've seen an epic rally over the last two weeks or so. In fact, the move has been so parabolic that it's something I have not witnessed since perhaps the Fed-juiced Coronavirus surge. Now, despite the enormous optimism in markets, there are still near-term uncertainties and the potential for a consolidation or a pullback phase.

Why There Could Be A Pullback/Consolidation Phase

Strictly from a technical perspective, stocks have rallied so much that many names and even major market averages have become considerably overbought here.

Just look at the S&P 500 "SPX" (SP500):

S&P 500 1-Year Chart

Two weeks ago "the sky was falling," yet now we're at new all-time highs "ATHs." The SPX has rallied from a low of roughly 6,340 to a recent high close to 7,080 (SPX futures), a highly impressive rally of roughly 12% in only about two weeks.

Now, this is not a typical rally. Nonetheless, that does not mean that the market won't go through a pullback/consolidation phase soon. The RSI has gone from below 30 roughly two weeks ago to around 70; an unprecedented reversal in sentiment and momentum in such a short time frame.

Uncertainties Still Persist

I may have overreacted slightly to the blockade, selling and decreasing several of my portfolio positions before the major run-ups over the last few days. However, after a reassessment of the blockade strategy.

I realized that while the move is escalatory in nature, it should actually lead to de-escalation in the general conflict. The Iranian economy is getting choked off without a reasonable response to the U.S.'s blockade strategy. Therefore, it may be a brilliant move on the side of the U.S., and while it may be risky, so far, it's going well.

Now, as Iran struggles for oil revenues, it may be more open to coming to the table and making a more reasonable deal with the U.S. over its uranium enrichment program, and other crucial sticking points. In short, Iran may have literally run out of cards, as its ultimate leverage strategy of extorting the Strait of Hormuz has been flipped on its head.

It was an unexpected and an extremely strong show of force, without having to blast power stations and blow up bridges, which would have made the situation much worse. Which is partly why the market has rallied so hard here recently.

Still, despite the parabolic price action in equities, uncertainties regarding the timing and the exact details of the Iran deal remain unclear. It does appear like the market is pricing in an ultra-bullish "base-case" scenario, and it's not a fact that things will go exactly to plan.

Additionally, there are earnings. The big banks have reported, with most providing better than anticipated EPS and revenue results. Still, there are some concerns regarding guidance and the potential for an increase in credit card, and potentially other defaults.

Also, there is the potential for increased inflation for longer, especially if a concrete agreement regarding the Strait is not achieved soon. The higher energy costs may also be weighing more on consumer sentiment and spending, as was shown in some recent numbers.

Consumer Sentiment Drops Like A Rock

Consumer sentiment actually hit its worst reading on record, a 47.6 level, well below the 51.6 consensus estimate. The current average (around a record high) 25% credit card rates in the U.S. are not helping consumers, many of whom remain heavily indebted in all sorts of debt (credit card, student loan, mortgages, auto loans, etc.).

Also, an interesting factor is that we do not typically see such negative consumer sentiment during healthy market cycles. Instead, we've seen lows of approximately 50 or so around recession bottoms, like in the early 1980s, 2009, 2011, and 2022, but why now?

It's possible that the consumer is in worse shape than the market perceives. It also appears like we are going through a potential decoupling of Wall Street and Main Street. Stocks are at ATHs again while the Main Street economy may be struggling, and the issues could worsen going forward.

Still No Clarity On The Fed

The market enjoys clarity, especially if it concerns monetary policy. Unfortunately, we are quite a bit away from a clear monetary perspective. When will the Fed cut interest rates? By how much? Could the Fed actually tighten rates before cutting?

It seems like almost anything is possible in this highly uncertain and still very volatile market environment. The market currently expects no more cuts this year. There is about a 2/3 probability that the Fed will hold the benchmark at its current rate going into next year. Subsequently, there is only about a 33% probability for one or maybe two rate cuts.

In general, the market appears relatively pessimistic on rate cuts, and I would prefer to see a shift toward a more dovish stance. However, this may require some time, a resolution to the Iran conflict, and a sustainable downtick in oil and other energy-related prices as we advance.

The Bottom Line

The market has had one of its most fierce short-term rallies I've seen in my 25 years of investing. Also, while earnings season got off to a mixed start, there is not a guarantee that tech earnings and guidance will be as stellar as the market anticipates.

Many companies could outperform, but I am becoming more concerned about the continued growth in the AI buildout process, as it could show more considerable signs of slowing as we advance. I am also concerned about the circular financing and whether companies like OpenAI and others will be able to meet their massive spending obligations in future years, especially if the credit markets tighten up.

In my opinion, no, they likely will not. This is not only concerning OpenAI, but also Anthropic, CoreWeave (CRWV), Nvidia (NVDA), and many other AI majors and supporting companies, which may inevitably fall victim to the eventual and likely inevitable unwind of the AI bubble. However, this may not occur in the near term, and for now, the AI segment may still have notable growth ahead.

Considering this scenario, I remain intermediate-term bullish while keeping a cautious outlook in the near term. Longer-term, there will likely be substantial issues, and a broad market downturn, bear market, and even a crash scenario could occur (possibly 2-5 years down the line). Provided that the Iran conflict may get resolved soon, the market has considerable constructive tailwinds. Thus, it is likely that we could still have a solid Q2 and send half to 2026.

While I recently decreased my SPX year-end target to a range of 7,300-7,500 on the blockade news (potential overreaction), I did not see the big picture entirely right away. Therefore, I am readjusting to a slightly higher year-end SPX PT range of 7,500-7,700, with a more cautious outlook for future years.

data.title: The Outlook For S&P 500 Dividends In April 2026
data.description:
How has the expected future changed for the quarterly dividends of the S&P 500 (SPX) in the month since our previous snapshot?

As of 15 April 2026, the outlook for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends through the end of 2026 and the first quarter of 2027 has improved considerably over the past month. Here is our summary of how the outlook for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share changed since our 13 March 2026 snapshot:

  • 2026-Q2: Increase of $0.21 to $19.77 per share
  • 2026-Q3: Increase of $0.25 to $20.48 per share
  • 2026-Q4: Increase of $0.27 to $20.38 per share
  • 2027-Q1: Increase of $0.55 to $21.74 per share

Not all the dividend futures data was rosy. On the day the dividend futures contract for 2026-Q1 ended on Friday, 20 March 2026, the expected dividend payout for 2026-Q1 plunged by $0.26 per share to a final value of $21.20 per share for the now-ended quarter.

The following chart shows how expectations for the S&P 500's quarterly dividends per share changed in the month from 13 March 2026 to 15 April 2026.

We're going to a rolling 13-quarter presentation for this chart, which, for this edition, covers 2024-Q2 through 2027-Q1 and gives us a first look at the dividend expectations for 2027-Q2. The first projection of that most distant future quarter of this outlook is $20.06 per share, which is $0.29 higher than the $19.77 per share expected for the current quarter of 2026-Q2.

If this is your first exposure to the S&P 500's quarterly dividend futures, be sure to read the following section that explains what this data is communicating about the future for the index's dividends.

More About Dividend Futures Data

For this series, we take a snapshot of the CME Group's S&P 500 quarterly dividend futures data shortly after the second or third week of each month.

Dividend futures indicate the amount of dividends per share to be paid out over the period covered by each quarter's dividend futures contracts, which start on the day after the preceding quarter's dividend futures contracts expire and end on the third Friday of the month ending the indicated quarter. For example, as determined by dividend futures contracts, the now "current" quarter of 2026-Q2 began on Saturday, 21 March 2026, and will end on Friday, 19 June 2026. Since the expectations for this quarter's dividend payouts can change all the way up to that final date, it qualifies as a future quarter.

Because dividend futures are tied to options contracts that run on this schedule, that makes these figures different from the quarterly dividends per share figures that are reported by Standard & Poor's. S&P reports the amount of dividends per share paid out during regular calendar quarters after the end of each quarter. This term mismatch accounts for the differences in dividends reported by both sources, with the biggest differences between the two typically seen in the first and fourth quarters of each year.

Dividend futures data is important for more than just what they project will be a future quarter's dividend payout. They represent the quantified expectations investors have for the future income they will realize from holding their investments, which affects how investors set current-day stock prices. How changes in the outlook for dividends at specific points of time in the future contribute to changes in current-day stock prices is described by this math.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: RENK Group: A Pure Play On Europe's Defense Boom
data.description:
RENK Group (RNKGF) stock has been mostly flat since my last report, contrasting with my buy rating and view that RENK Group could be among the beneficiaries of Europe's push for security autonomy. The stock already lost steam in 2025 after the capital markets day in which the company showed that there would be little revenue growth acceleration in the near term. The war in Iran has not strengthened sentiment, as investors are increasingly growing aware that near-term demand is robust and the war only adds input cost concerns. In this report, I discuss RENK Group's FY2025 results and update my price target in support of my buy rating.

Strong Growth For RENK Group In 2025

In 2025, RENK Group saw its revenues increase 20% to €1.37 billion, with defense sales growing from 72% of revenues to 74% of revenues. New build sales now account for 64% of revenues compared to 60% a year ago, with the remainder attributable to aftermarket sales. Adjusted EBIT grew 22% to €230 million, with margins increasing 30 basis points to 16.9%. We see that margin impact is modest, but if we consider that the company has to invest to grow the business and ramp up production and is seeing sales shift somewhat from higher-margin aftermarket sales to lower-margin new build sales, I do believe that a 30 basis point expansion is actually quite good. Order intake grew 9% to €1.6 billion with a book-to-bill ratio of 1.15x, which shows that there is not a lot of near-term opportunity to accelerate revenue growth, or at least it is not captured in the backlog yet.

Defense revenues grew 24% to a little over €1 billion, with order intake growing 4% to €1.17 billion. We note that some expected program awards for navy programs and main battle tank programs shifted into 2026. If those had occurred in 2025, the order intake would have been €1.37 billion.

Vehicle Mobility, which includes tracked and heavy military vehicles, grew revenues by 24.8% to €872 million, with EBIT increasing 27.8% to €178 million. Margins expanded 40 basis points to 20.4%. Order intake grew 11% to €1.13 billion, indicating a 1.3x book-to-bill ratio. Marine & Industrial sales grew 15.3% to €580 million with 30% EBIT growth to €45 million and margins growing strongly from 10.6% to 11.9%. Order intake grew 6.3% to €327 million, bringing the book-to-bill ratio to 0.86x. Slide bearing sales grew 2.5% to €128 million, with EBIT growing 6.9% to €23 million, indicating a 70-basis point margin expansion to 17.9%. Order intake declined 5% to €126 million with a book-to-bill ratio of around 1.

RENK Group Guides for Around 10% Sales Growth and Margin Expansion

For RENK Group, the defense sales are the growth driver and ramping up production should deliver margin expansion. For 2026, the company guides for more than €1.5 billion in sales, which would indicate around 10% growth in sales. The company is guiding for adjusted EBIT between €255 million and €285 million. This would indicate earnings growth between 10.9% and 24%. The derived margins from sales and earnings guidance would be 17%-19% compared to 16.8% a year ago. So, the company is marching towards its 2030 targets, which are €2.8-€3 billion in sales with margins over 20%, but especially on sales, the company needs to keep delivering year after year.

RENK Group Has Significant Upside

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Values are converted from EUR to USD. RENK Group has two OTC tickers; the first one is RNKGF, representing the ordinary shares, and the second one is RKGRY, representing 0.2 ordinary shares. The price targets are based on the ordinary shares, meaning that to obtain the price target for RKGRY, you simply have to divide the stated price targets by five. We do note that RNKGF has the higher trading volume. Given that higher volumes and liquidity provide better chances to buy and sell at desired prices and in desired quantities, I do believe that RNKGF provides the better option out of the two to invest in RENK Group. Alternatively, we note that the R3NK ticker in Frankfurt provides even higher volumes.

For RENK Group, I am maintaining my buy rating with a base case target of $74.44, indicating 18% upside, and a more optimistic target of $81.29, indicating 29% upside. We note that this upside is based on expected earnings for 2026. Usually, I value stocks a year ahead of earnings, so in this case that would be by looking at expected earnings and cash flows for 2027. However, for RENK Group, we see that even this year's earnings are not fully reflected in the stock price yet. Expected sales growth is 16.4% annually through 2028, with 21.6% growth in EBITDA and 30.5% growth in free cash flow.

For RENK Group, the expectations are that margins will significantly increase, and that is driven by the company increasing its volumes, which drives operating leverage. In the years ahead, operating and free cash flow will grow. We note that the free cash flow in 2026 benefits from some contracts slipping from 2025 into 2026. CapEx is expected to peak this year at 5% of sales. The company is expected to increase its dividends in the years ahead, but that does not drive an impressive yield. However, over the longer term it could be a good yield on cost, though RENK Group is likely a stock you should buy for the potential upside, and the dividend is just a "nice to have". Net debt is expected to substantially decrease as the company's free cash flow accelerates. In terms of net debt leverage, we see that the company is in a comfortable position.

Conclusion: RENK Group Operating Leverage Should Drive the Business

I believe that RENK Group has bright prospects as the company ramps up its production. Production ramp-ups come with some initial margin downside but ultimately drive operating leverage, and for RENK Group, the improvements should be notable in the EBITDA margin expansion, growing EBITDA, and free cash flows. I believe that the stock remains a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
